categories.name: Process Monitoring
categories.description: Monitor and track running processes in real-time

categories.name: Process Discovery & Investigation
categories.description: Find, inspect, and manage individual processes

categories.name: GPU Monitoring & Debugging
categories.description: Monitor NVIDIA GPU utilization, memory, and processes

categories.name: Memory Monitoring
categories.description: Track RAM usage, swap, and memory statistics

categories.name: File & Port Debugging
categories.description: Investigate open files, locked resources, and network ports

categories.description: Track logged-in users and session activity

categories.description: Monitor disk space, directory sizes, and storage usage

categories.description: Read, tail, and search through system and application logs

categories.description: Search files, navigate codebases, and locate content

categories.description: Diagnose network connectivity, DNS, and routing issues

categories.name: Docker Debugging
categories.description: Manage and debug Docker containers, images, and services

categories.name: AI/ML Specific Commands
categories.description: Commands tailored for AI/ML workflows, training, and inference

categories.name: Conda Environment Management
categories.description: List, inspect, and analyze Conda environments and their disk usage

categories.name: Most Important Commands
categories.description: Essential commands every AI/ML engineer should know — curated from all categories

commands.name: Find a Process
commands.purpose: Search for running processes by name or keyword.
usefulWhen: Checking if a service is running
commands.notes: The grep itself may appear in the results. Use `ps aux | grep [v]llm` or pipe through `grep -v grep` to filter it out.

commands.name: Check Process Owner and Parent
commands.purpose: Display the user, PID, parent PID, and command for a specific process.
usefulWhen: Auditing process ownership on shared servers
commands.notes: You can customize the output columns by changing the -o fields (e.g., add %cpu,%mem).

commands.name: View Full Command
commands.purpose: Display the full command used to launch a process.
usefulWhen: Understanding how a process was started
Finding model paths in ML training runs
commands.notes: Use `ps -efp PID` for even more detailed output including environment variables.

commands.name: Live Process Monitoring
commands.purpose: Real-time monitoring of CPU, RAM, and running processes.
commands.notes: Press 'q' to quit. Press 'P' to sort by CPU, 'M' to sort by memory, '1' to show per-core CPU usage.

commands.name: Advanced Process Monitoring
commands.purpose: Interactive, visual process monitor — an enhanced alternative to top.
usefulWhen: Sorting and filtering by CPU or RAM
Getting a better visual overview of system resources
commands.notes: Supports mouse interaction. Press F5 for tree view, F6 to sort, F9 to kill a process.

commands.name: Find Process by Name
commands.purpose: Search for processes by name — a cleaner alternative to ps aux | grep.
usefulWhen: Quickly finding if a specific process is running
Listing all matching processes with full command lines
commands.notes: The -a flag shows the full command line, -f matches against the full command string, not just the process name.

commands.name: Find PID of a Program
commands.purpose: Returns the process ID(s) for a running program by its exact name.
usefulWhen: Getting PID for scripting and automation
Checking if a program has multiple instances
Feeding PID to kill or other commands
commands.notes: Returns multiple PIDs if several instances are running. Returns nothing (exit code 1) if the process is not found.

commands.name: Gracefully Stop a Process
commands.purpose: Send a SIGTERM signal to gracefully terminate a process.
usefulWhen: Allowing a process to save state before exiting
Terminating training runs that need cleanup
commands.notes: SIGTERM allows the process to catch the signal and perform cleanup. If the process doesn't respond, use kill -9.

commands.name: Force Kill a Process
commands.purpose: Immediately and forcefully terminate a process with SIGKILL.
usefulWhen: Process is unresponsive to normal kill
Freeing GPU memory held by a stuck training job
commands.notes: SIGKILL cannot be caught or ignored by the process. Use as a last resort — the process won't get a chance to clean up.

commands.name: Start a Low-Priority Process
commands.purpose: Launch a process with a lower CPU scheduling priority.
usefulWhen: Running background tasks on shared servers
Preventing a training job from hogging all CPU
Being courteous on multi-user GPU machines
commands.notes: Nice values range from -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest priority). Default is 0. Only root can set negative nice values.

commands.name: Change Priority of Running Process
commands.purpose: Adjust the CPU scheduling priority of an already running process.
usefulWhen: Lowering priority of a runaway process
Adjusting resource allocation on shared servers
commands.notes: Regular users can only increase the nice value (lower priority). Root can decrease it (raise priority).

commands.name: View Parent-Child Tree
commands.purpose: Show the full process tree from init to the specified PID, including PIDs.
usefulWhen: Tracing which parent spawned a child process
commands.notes: The -s flag shows ancestors up to init, -p shows PIDs. Add -a to show command-line arguments.

commands.name: GPU Status Overview
commands.purpose: Display NVIDIA GPU status including utilization, memory, temperature, and running processes.
usefulWhen: Checking GPU memory usage before launching a model
Identifying which processes are using GPUs
commands.notes: Comes pre-installed with NVIDIA drivers. Shows GPU index, name, fan speed, temperature, power, memory usage, and active processes.

commands.name: Live GPU Monitoring
commands.purpose: Continuously refresh nvidia-smi output every second for real-time GPU monitoring.
usefulWhen: Monitoring GPU usage during model training
commands.notes: Press Ctrl+C to stop. Change interval with -n flag (e.g., watch -n 5 for every 5 seconds). Use watch -d to highlight changes.

commands.name: Per-Process GPU Monitoring
commands.purpose: Monitor per-process GPU utilization and memory usage in real-time.
usefulWhen: Identifying which process is consuming GPU resources
Monitoring per-process GPU compute and memory
Debugging GPU resource contention between users
commands.notes: Use -i to specify GPU index (0-based). Shows PID, type (C=Compute, G=Graphics), SM%, memory%, encoder%, decoder% per process.

commands.name: Find Processes Using GPU
commands.purpose: List all processes that have the NVIDIA GPU device files open.
usefulWhen: Finding hidden processes using GPU memory
Clearing GPU before launching a new job
commands.notes: The -v flag provides verbose output showing USER, PID, ACCESS, and COMMAND. Useful when nvidia-smi doesn't show all GPU-using processes.

commands.name: Query GPU Properties
commands.purpose: Extract specific GPU metrics in a CSV-friendly format for scripting and logging.
usefulWhen: Logging GPU metrics to a file
Automating GPU health checks in scripts
commands.notes: Available fields: name, temperature.gpu, power.draw, memory.total, memory.used, memory.free, utilization.gpu, utilization.memory, and many more. Run `nvidia-smi --help-query-gpu` for full list.

commands.name: Interactive GPU Monitor
commands.purpose: An interactive, htop-like GPU process and resource monitor with rich visualization.
usefulWhen: Wanting a prettier GPU monitoring interface
commands.notes: Provides colored output, per-process GPU stats, and a much more user-friendly interface than nvidia-smi. Supports filtering and sorting.

commands.name: Check Memory Usage
commands.purpose: Display system RAM and swap usage in human-readable format.
usefulWhen: Checking available RAM before loading a large model
Monitoring swap usage on GPU servers
commands.notes: The -h flag shows values in GB/MB. 'available' column is more accurate than 'free' for estimating usable memory.

commands.name: Virtual Memory Statistics
commands.purpose: Report virtual memory, CPU, and I/O statistics with periodic updates.
usefulWhen: Monitoring I/O wait and context switches
commands.notes: First line shows averages since boot. Subsequent lines show real-time stats. Key columns: si/so (swap in/out), bi/bo (block I/O), wa (I/O wait).

commands.name: Detailed Memory Info
commands.purpose: Display detailed kernel memory statistics directly from the proc filesystem.
usefulWhen: Checking HugePages configuration for ML workloads
commands.notes: Contains detailed fields like MemTotal, MemFree, MemAvailable, Buffers, Cached, SwapTotal, SwapFree, HugePages_Total, etc.

commands.name: List Open Files by Process
commands.purpose: List all files opened by a specific process.
usefulWhen: Finding which files a process is reading/writing
Checking model file access during inference
commands.notes: On Linux, everything is a file — lsof shows regular files, sockets, pipes, devices, and more.

commands.name: Find Process Using a Port
commands.purpose: Identify which process is listening on or connected to a specific network port.
usefulWhen: Finding what's running on a vLLM or API port
Checking if a model server started correctly
commands.notes: Use `lsof -i :8000 -t` to get only the PID. Combine with kill for quick cleanup: `kill $(lsof -i :8000 -t)`.

commands.name: Find Process on Port (fuser)
commands.purpose: Quickly identify which process is using a specific TCP port.
usefulWhen: Killing a process blocking a port
commands.notes: Use `fuser -k 8000/tcp` to immediately kill the process using the port. Add -v for verbose output.

commands.name: List Listening Ports
commands.purpose: Show all TCP and UDP listening ports with process information.
usefulWhen: Finding all listening ports on a server
Checking if a model API server is bound correctly
commands.notes: Flags: -t (TCP), -u (UDP), -l (listening), -p (process info), -n (numeric ports). ss is the modern replacement for netstat.

commands.name: List Listening Ports (netstat)
commands.purpose: Show all listening TCP/UDP ports with associated processes — legacy alternative to ss.
usefulWhen: Working on older Linux systems without ss
commands.notes: netstat is deprecated on modern systems in favor of ss. The net-tools package may not be installed by default.

commands.name: Show Logged-in Users
commands.purpose: Display currently logged-in users, their terminals, and login times.
usefulWhen: Checking who else is on the GPU server
commands.notes: Shows username, terminal (tty/pts), login date/time, and source IP for remote sessions.

commands.name: Detailed User Activity
commands.purpose: Show logged-in users with system uptime, load average, and what they're running.
usefulWhen: Seeing what commands other users are running
Checking system load and uptime at a glance
Identifying idle vs active user sessions
commands.notes: Combines info from who, uptime, and process monitoring. IDLE column shows how long a user has been inactive.

commands.name: Login History
commands.purpose: Show the history of user logins and reboots.
usefulWhen: Auditing who accessed the server and when
commands.notes: Use `last -n 20` to show only the last 20 entries. `last reboot` shows only system reboots. Data comes from /var/log/wtmp.

commands.name: Check Disk Space
commands.purpose: Show disk space usage for all mounted filesystems in human-readable format.
usefulWhen: Checking if disk is full before downloading a model
Debugging 'No space left on device' errors
commands.notes: Use `df -h /path` to check a specific mount point. The -i flag shows inode usage instead of disk space.

commands.name: Check Directory Size
commands.purpose: Show the total size of a directory or file in human-readable format.
usefulWhen: Finding which directories are using the most space
commands.notes: Use `du -sh *` to show sizes of all items in the current directory. Add `| sort -h` to sort by size.

commands.name: Interactive Disk Usage Analyzer
commands.purpose: Interactive, ncurses-based disk usage analyzer for exploring directory sizes.
usefulWhen: Interactively exploring which directories use the most space
Cleaning up disk space on GPU servers
Finding large model files and datasets
commands.notes: Navigate with arrow keys, press 'd' to delete files/directories. Use -x to stay on a single filesystem.

commands.name: Follow Log File
commands.purpose: Continuously display new lines appended to a log file in real-time.
usefulWhen: Following vLLM or inference server logs
commands.notes: Use `tail -f -n 100` to start with the last 100 lines. Combine with grep: `tail -f log.txt | grep ERROR`.

commands.name: View System Journal
commands.purpose: Query and display logs from the systemd journal with explanations.
usefulWhen: Debugging service crashes (e.g., Docker, NVIDIA driver)
commands.notes: Flags: -x (add explanatory text), -e (jump to end). Use `-u <service>` to filter by service, `--since '1 hour ago'` for time ranges, `-f` to follow.

commands.name: Kernel Messages
commands.purpose: Display recent kernel ring buffer messages — essential for hardware debugging.
commands.notes: Use `dmesg -T` for human-readable timestamps. `dmesg | grep -i gpu` to filter GPU-related messages. May require sudo on some systems.

commands.name: Search Logs for Errors
commands.purpose: Recursively search through log files for specific error patterns.
usefulWhen: Hunting for specific error messages across log files
Finding OOM or CUDA errors in system logs
commands.notes: Use -i for case-insensitive search, -n for line numbers, -l for filenames only. Combine patterns: `grep -r 'error\|fail\|OOM' /var/log/`.

commands.name: Find Files
commands.purpose: Search for files and directories by name, type, size, date, and more.
usefulWhen: Searching for large files to clean up
commands.notes: Useful flags: -type f (files only), -type d (dirs only), -size +100M (large files), -mtime -7 (modified in last 7 days), -exec (run command on results).

commands.name: Fast File Lookup
commands.purpose: Instantly find files using a pre-built database index — much faster than find.
usefulWhen: Quickly finding a file by name
commands.installation: sudo apt install mlocate && sudo updatedb
commands.notes: Database is updated periodically via cron. Run `sudo updatedb` to refresh manually. Won't find files created since last updatedb.

commands.name: Search File Contents
commands.purpose: Recursively search for text patterns inside files with line numbers.
usefulWhen: Finding where a configuration value is defined
Searching for function definitions in codebases
commands.notes: Use -i for case-insensitive, --include='*.py' to restrict to Python files. Consider using ripgrep (rg) for faster searches in large codebases.

commands.name: Directory Tree View
commands.purpose: Display directory structure as a visual tree with indentation.
usefulWhen: Getting an overview of ML project organization
commands.notes: Use -L to limit depth, -I to exclude patterns (e.g., tree -I '__pycache__|node_modules'), -d for directories only.

commands.name: Count Lines in Files
commands.purpose: Count the number of lines in one or more files.
usefulWhen: Counting lines in training data files
commands.notes: Use `find . -name '*.py' | xargs wc -l` to count lines across all Python files. `wc -w` counts words, `wc -c` counts bytes.

commands.name: Test Network Connectivity
commands.purpose: Send ICMP packets to test network connectivity and measure latency.
usefulWhen: Checking if a remote server is reachable
Measuring network latency to cloud services
Testing connectivity to model download servers
commands.notes: Use `ping -c 4` to send only 4 packets. On Linux, ping runs indefinitely by default (Ctrl+C to stop).

commands.name: Test HTTP Endpoint
commands.purpose: Make HTTP requests to test APIs, download files, or check endpoint health.
usefulWhen: Testing if a model API is responding
Checking HTTP headers and status codes
commands.notes: Flags: -I (headers only), -o (output file), -s (silent), -v (verbose), -X POST (POST request), -d (data). Use `curl -o /dev/null -s -w '%{http_code}' URL` for just status code.

commands.name: Trace Network Route
commands.purpose: Show the network path (hops) packets take to reach a destination.
usefulWhen: Identifying routing issues to cloud providers
Debugging high latency to remote GPU clusters
commands.notes: Some hops may show '* * *' due to firewalls blocking ICMP. Use `traceroute -T` for TCP-based traceroute to work around this.

commands.name: DNS Lookup
commands.purpose: Perform DNS lookups to resolve domain names and debug DNS issues.
usefulWhen: Checking if a domain resolves correctly
commands.notes: Use `dig +short` for concise output. `dig @8.8.8.8 domain` to query a specific DNS server. `dig -x IP` for reverse DNS.

commands.name: Show Network Interfaces
commands.purpose: Display all network interfaces with their IP addresses and configuration.
usefulWhen: Verifying InfiniBand or high-speed network setup
commands.notes: Modern replacement for ifconfig. Use `ip -brief addr` for a compact view. `ip route` shows the routing table.

commands.name: List Running Containers
commands.purpose: Show all running Docker containers with their status, ports, and names.
usefulWhen: Checking which ML services are running
commands.notes: Use `docker ps -a` to show all containers including stopped ones. `docker ps --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}'` for custom output.

commands.name: View Container Logs
commands.purpose: Display stdout/stderr logs from a Docker container.
usefulWhen: Viewing training output from a container
commands.notes: Use `-f` to follow logs in real-time (like tail -f). `--tail 100` for last 100 lines. `--since 1h` for last hour of logs.

commands.name: Inspect Container Details
commands.purpose: Show detailed JSON metadata about a container including network, volumes, and environment.
commands.notes: Output is JSON. Use jq to filter: `docker inspect container | jq '.[0].NetworkSettings.IPAddress'`. Check GPU access: `docker inspect container | jq '.[0].HostConfig.DeviceRequests'`.

commands.name: Container Resource Usage
commands.purpose: Display real-time resource usage (CPU, memory, network, I/O) for all running containers.
usefulWhen: Monitoring container CPU and memory consumption
Checking network I/O for model serving containers
commands.notes: Use `docker stats --no-stream` for a single snapshot. `docker stats container_name` to monitor a specific container.

commands.name: Execute Command in Container
commands.purpose: Open an interactive shell or run a command inside a running Docker container.
usefulWhen: Debugging inside a running ML container
Checking installed Python packages in a container
Inspecting model files inside a container
commands.notes: The -i flag keeps stdin open, -t allocates a pseudo-TTY. Use `docker exec container_name command` for non-interactive execution.

commands.name: Docker Compose Logs
commands.purpose: Follow logs from a specific service in a Docker Compose stack.
usefulWhen: Following logs from a specific compose service
commands.notes: Use `docker-compose logs` without service name to see all services. Modern Docker uses `docker compose` (without hyphen) as a subcommand.

commands.name: Docker Disk Usage
commands.purpose: Show Docker disk usage broken down by images, containers, volumes, and build cache.
usefulWhen: Investigating why Docker is using so much disk space
Identifying reclaimable storage from unused images
Cleaning up Docker resources on GPU servers
Monitoring Docker storage on shared machines
commands.notes: Use `docker system df -v` for detailed breakdown. Clean up with `docker system prune` (removes stopped containers, dangling images, unused networks).

commands.name: List Python Packages
commands.purpose: List all installed Python packages and their versions.
commands.notes: Use `pip list --outdated` to show packages with newer versions. `pip show package_name` for detailed info about a specific package.

commands.name: List Conda Packages
commands.purpose: List all packages in the active Conda environment.
usefulWhen: Checking CUDA toolkit version in Conda
commands.notes: Use `conda list -n env_name` to list packages from a specific environment. `conda env export > environment.yml` to export the full environment.

commands.name: Verify PyTorch CUDA Access
commands.purpose: Quickly verify that PyTorch can access CUDA GPUs and count available devices.
usefulWhen: Checking GPU access after driver updates
commands.notes: Returns True/False for CUDA availability and the number of GPUs. Also try: `python -c "import torch; print(torch.version.cuda)"` to check CUDA version.

commands.name: Monitor GPU During Training
commands.purpose: Continuously monitor GPU utilization and memory during model training or inference.
usefulWhen: Detecting GPU utilization drops (data loading bottlenecks)
Watching for OOM during model loading
commands.notes: Look for: GPU-Util% (should be high during training), Memory-Usage (watch for OOM), and Temperature (thermal throttling above 80°C).

commands.name: Compact GPU Status
commands.purpose: Show a compact, colorized summary of GPU status — a simpler alternative to nvidia-smi.
usefulWhen: Getting a quick GPU overview in one line per GPU
Monitoring GPU usage from multiple SSH sessions
Checking which user is using which GPU
commands.notes: Flags: -c (color), -u (show users), -p (show PID). Use `watch gpustat -cup` for continuous monitoring. Much more readable than nvidia-smi for daily use.

commands.name: Top 10 Largest Directories
commands.purpose: Find the top 10 largest directories in your home folder, sorted by size.
usefulWhen: Figuring out what's eating disk space

commands.name: Check HuggingFace Cache Size
commands.purpose: Check the disk space used by downloaded HuggingFace model weights and datasets.
usefulWhen: Running out of disk space on ML servers
Before downloading a new large model
Auditing storage usage across team members
commands.notes: HuggingFace stores model weights in ~/.cache/huggingface/hub/. Large models like LLaMA or Mistral can use 50-200GB each.

commands.name: Clean Pip Cache
commands.purpose: Remove all cached pip packages to free up disk space.
usefulWhen: Freeing disk space on ML servers
commands.notes: Can free up 10+ GB if you've done many package installs. Check cache size first with `pip cache info`.

commands.name: Clean Conda Cache
commands.purpose: Remove unused Conda packages, tarballs, and cache to reclaim disk space.
usefulWhen: Freeing disk space on conda environments
commands.notes: The -y flag skips confirmation prompts. Use `conda clean --packages` to only remove unused packages.

commands.name: Check Disk Space for Home
commands.purpose: Display disk space usage for the /home partition in human-readable format.
usefulWhen: Checking available space before downloading models
Debugging 'No space left on device' errors

commands.name: Check PID Start Time
commands.purpose: Display the exact start date and time of a specific process by its PID.
usefulWhen: Checking how long a training job has been running
commands.notes: The = after lstart= suppresses the column header. You can also use -o etime= for elapsed time since start.

commands.name: List All Conda Environments
commands.purpose: Display a list of all Conda environments with their paths.
usefulWhen: Checking how many Conda environments exist
Finding the path to a specific environment
Auditing environments on a shared server
commands.notes: The active environment is marked with an asterisk (*). Equivalent to `conda info --envs`.

commands.name: Conda Environment Info
commands.purpose: Show all available Conda environments with their installation paths.
usefulWhen: Listing environments and their full paths
Verifying which environment is currently active
commands.notes: Same output as `conda env list`. Use `conda info` (without --envs) for full Conda installation details.

commands.name: Check Size of Each Conda Environment
commands.purpose: Show the disk space used by each individual Conda environment.
usefulWhen: Finding which Conda environment is consuming the most space
Auditing storage usage on shared GPU servers
commands.notes: Replace ~/miniconda3 with your actual Conda installation path (e.g., ~/anaconda3). Check with `conda info --base`.

commands.name: Sort Conda Environments by Size
commands.purpose: List all Conda environments sorted by size from largest to smallest.
usefulWhen: Identifying the largest environments for cleanup
Prioritizing which environments to remove first
Quick disk space triage on ML servers
commands.notes: The -h flag makes sort understand human-readable sizes (e.g., 10G > 500M). The -r flag reverses to descending order.

commands.name: Total Size of All Conda Environments
commands.purpose: Show the combined total disk space used by all Conda environments.
usefulWhen: Getting a quick overview of total Conda storage usage
Reporting environment disk usage to team or admin
Comparing Conda footprint vs other storage consumers
commands.notes: This gives a single total number. Use `du -sh ~/miniconda3/envs/*` to see individual environment sizes.

commands.name: List Conda Environments with Sizes
commands.command: conda env list | grep -v '^#' | awk '{print $NF}' | xargs du -sh 2>/dev/null
commands.purpose: Display every Conda environment alongside its total storage size.
commands.example: conda env list | grep -v '^#' | awk '{print $NF}' | xargs du -sh 2>/dev/null
usefulWhen: Getting a human-readable list of all environments with sizes
Auditing environment storage on GPU servers
Creating reports on Conda disk usage
commands.syntax: conda env list | grep -v '^#' | awk '{print $NF}' | xargs du -sh 2>/dev/null
commands.notes: This pipes the output of conda env list through awk to extract paths, then runs du -sh on each. The 2>/dev/null suppresses permission errors.

commands.name: Miniconda Installation Disk Breakdown
commands.purpose: Find which specific folders inside your Miniconda installation are consuming the most disk space.
usefulWhen: Comparing envs vs package cache sizes
commands.notes: Common large directories: envs/ (virtual environments), pkgs/ (downloaded packages cache). Run `conda clean --all -y` to clear the pkgs cache.

commands.name: Home Directory Space Usage
commands.purpose: Show which folders in your home directory are consuming the most space, sorted largest first.
usefulWhen: Finding what is consuming disk space in your home folder
Identifying large model checkpoints, datasets, or caches
Monitoring personal disk usage on shared servers
commands.notes: The --max-depth=1 flag limits output to immediate subdirectories. Remove the flag to see deeper nesting. Pipe to `head -n 20` for top 20 only.

commands.name: Check Any Folder Size
commands.purpose: Check the total size of any specific folder on the filesystem.
usefulWhen: Checking the size of a dataset directory
Estimating storage before copying or transferring files
commands.notes: Use -s for summary (total only) and -h for human-readable sizes. Without -s, du shows every subdirectory individually.

commands.name: List Subfolder Sizes
commands.purpose: Show the size of every subfolder and file inside a given directory.
usefulWhen: Finding the largest subfolders inside a directory
Inspecting which project or dataset takes the most space
Breaking down storage usage within a specific path
commands.notes: The * glob expands to all items in the directory. Add `| sort -hr` to sort by size descending. Hidden files (starting with .) are not included unless you also add du -sh .* .
